content:
# Exodus Chapter 36 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 107–108; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 79–80 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_036_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_036_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_036_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_036_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Work Begins — More Than Enough (vv. 1–7)

**1** So Bezalel, Oholiab, and every skilled person to whom YHWH had given wisdom and understanding to know how to carry out all the work of constructing the sanctuary set to work doing everything that YHWH had commanded. **2** Moses summoned Bezalel, Oholiab, and every skilled person whose heart YHWH had filled with ability — everyone whose heart moved them to come forward and take part in the work. **3** They received from Moses all the contributions that the Sons of Israel had brought for the work of constructing the sanctuary. But the people kept bringing additional freewill offerings morning after morning. **4** So all the skilled workers who were doing the various tasks on the sanctuary left what they were working on **5** and came to Moses, saying, "The people are bringing far more than is needed for the work that YHWH has commanded us to do." **6** Moses then gave an order, and they sent this announcement throughout the camp: "No man or woman should prepare any more material as a contribution for the sanctuary." So the people were restrained from bringing more. **7** The material they already had was more than enough for all the work — and then some.

---

### The Inner Curtains of the Tabernacle (vv. 8–13)

**8** All the most skilled workers among those doing the work made the tabernacle out of ten curtain panels of finely twisted linen, with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn. They worked cherubim into the fabric with the skill of a master designer. **9** Each curtain panel was forty-two feet long and six feet wide — all the panels were identical in size. **10** They joined five of the curtain panels together as one set, and did the same with the other five. **11** They made loops of blue yarn along the edge of the last panel in the first set, and did the same along the edge of the last panel in the second set. **12** They made fifty loops on the first curtain panel and fifty matching loops on the corresponding panel of the second set, with the loops positioned directly opposite each other. **13** Then they made fifty gold clasps and used them to fasten the two sets of curtains together, so that the tabernacle formed a single unified structure.

---

### The Goat-Hair Tent Covering (vv. 14–18)

**14** They also made curtain panels of goat hair as a tent covering over the tabernacle — eleven panels in all. **15** Each panel was forty-five feet long and six feet wide — all eleven were identical in size. **16** They joined five of the panels together as one set, and the remaining six as another set. **17** They made fifty loops along the edge of the last panel in the first set and fifty loops along the edge of the corresponding panel in the second set. **18** They made fifty bronze clasps to fasten the tent together as a single unit.

---

### The Outer Coverings (v. 19)

**19** Then they made a covering for the tent out of ram skins dyed red, and over that an additional covering of fine leather.

---

### The Upright Frames (vv. 20–30)

**20** They made the upright frames for the tabernacle out of acacia wood. **21** Each frame was fifteen feet tall and two and a quarter feet wide, **22** with two tenons joined to each other. They made all the frames of the tabernacle this way. **23** They made the frames for the tabernacle as follows: twenty frames for the south side, **24** with forty silver bases under the twenty frames — two bases under each frame, one for each tenon. **25** For the second side of the tabernacle, the north side, they made twenty frames **26** and forty silver bases — two bases under each frame. **27** For the far end of the tabernacle, on the west, they made six frames, **28** and they made two frames for the corners of the tabernacle at the far end. **29** These corner frames were joined together from the bottom all the way to the top, fitted into a single ring. They made both corner frames the same way. **30** So there were eight frames with their silver bases — sixteen bases in all, two under each frame.

---

### The Crossbars (vv. 31–34)

**31** They made crossbars of acacia wood — five for the frames on one side of the tabernacle, **32** five for the frames on the other side, and five for the frames at the far end on the west. **33** They made the middle crossbar so that it ran from end to end, halfway up the frames. **34** They overlaid the frames with gold, made gold rings to hold the crossbars, and overlaid the crossbars with gold as well.

---

### The Inner Curtain and the Entrance Screen (vv. 35–38)

**35** They made the inner curtain out of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and finely twisted linen, with cherubim worked into it by a master designer. **36** They made four posts of acacia wood for it, overlaid them with gold, and fitted them with gold hooks, and cast four silver bases for them. **37** For the entrance to the tent they made a screen of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and finely twisted linen, the work of an embroiderer, **38** along with its five posts and their hooks. They overlaid the tops of the posts and their bands with gold, and made five bronze bases for them.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Verse 1 — "Set to work"
The Hebrew וְעָשָׂה (ve'asah) — "and he shall work/do" — transitions the narrative from the gathering of materials (ch. 35) to the actual construction. Bezalel and Oholiab are named first, but the text immediately broadens to include כָּל־אִישׁ חֲכַם־לֵב (kol ish chakham-lev, "every person wise of heart") to whom YHWH gave skill. The LXX in Vaticanus presents this section in a substantially different arrangement from the MT — the Greek tradition reorganizes the tabernacle construction chapters significantly, and what the MT presents in ch. 36 is partially paralleled by the Vaticanus text in its own version of the construction sequence (corresponding roughly to LXX Exodus 36–37 in the Greek numbering, which maps to ch. 26 of the instruction narrative). The dynamic rendering "set to work" captures the decisive shift from preparation to execution.

### Verses 3–7 — The surplus
This passage is one of the most remarkable in the Hebrew Bible. The Hebrew phrase דֵּי הַמְּלָאכָה (dei hammelakhah, "enough for the work") is intensified with the addition of וְהוֹתֵר (vehoter, "and surplus/leftover") in v. 7 — they had not merely enough, but more than enough. The verb הוּשְׁבְּתוּ (hushbetu, "they were restrained") in v. 6 uses the same root as שַׁבָּת (shabbat) — the people had to be made to cease, to sabbath from their giving. The LXX reads καὶ ἐκωλύθη ὁ λαός (and the people were prevented). Moses must issue a command — sent through the camp as a public proclamation — to stop the people from bringing more. This is the only place in Scripture where the leadership must restrain the congregation from excessive generosity. The dynamic rendering "far more than is needed" and "and then some" captures the extravagance that overflowed the need. The theological resonance is powerful: these are the same people who had so recently given their gold for the golden calf (32:2–4). Their generosity for the true sanctuary so exceeds what is needed that it must be forcibly stopped.

### Verse 8 — "Master designer"
The Hebrew מַעֲשֵׂה חֹשֵׁב (ma'aseh choshev) — literally "the work of a thinker/designer" — describes the highest category of textile art. As noted at 35:35, this refers to figured weaving where the design appears on both sides of the fabric, as distinct from רֹקֵם (roqem, embroidery/needlework) where the design appears only on one side. The cherubim woven into these innermost curtains — the ones facing the interior of the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place — were the work of this most skilled technique. The LXX renders ἔργον ὑφάντου (work of a weaver). The dynamic rendering "master designer" captures the intellectual and artistic dimension of חֹשֵׁב — this was not mere handwork but design-work, the expression of a creative mind filled with God's Spirit.

### Verses 9–13 — Measurements and construction
The Hebrew measurements are given in cubits: each curtain was twenty-eight cubits long and four cubits wide. A cubit (אַמָּה, ammah) is approximately 18 inches (45 cm), yielding approximately 42 feet by 6 feet. The ten panels, joined in two sets of five and clasped together with fifty gold קְרָסִים (qerasim, clasps/hooks), formed the innermost layer of the tabernacle — the one visible from inside the sacred space. The gold clasps that joined them ensured the whole structure was, in the Hebrew phrase, הַמִּשְׁכָּן אֶחָד (hammishkan echad, "the tabernacle, one") — a single unified dwelling. The LXX reads ἵνα ᾖ ἡ σκηνὴ μία (so that the tent might be one). The insistence on unity — one tabernacle, not two halves — carries theological weight: YHWH's dwelling among his people is singular and undivided.

### Verses 14–18 — Goat hair and bronze
The goat-hair panels — eleven rather than ten, each thirty cubits by four (approximately 45 feet by 6 feet) — formed the second layer, draped over the inner curtains. The extra panel provided overhang. Where the inner curtains were joined by gold clasps, these were joined by bronze (נְחֹשֶׁת, nechoshet) clasps — a descending hierarchy of materials from interior to exterior mirroring the graded holiness of the space itself. The LXX preserves the same structural pattern. The goat hair (עִזִּים, izzim) was the material spun by those specially moved women of 35:26 — their exacting work now takes tangible form as the protective covering of YHWH's dwelling.

### Verse 19 — Two outer coverings
The Hebrew mentions two additional layers: אֵילִם מְאָדָּמִים (eilim me'oddamim, "rams dyed red") and תְּחָשִׁים (techashim). The identity of תְּחָשׁ remains debated — proposals include dugong/sea-cow skin, fine leather, dolphin hide, and even a specific color or treatment process. The LXX renders δέρματα κριῶν ἠρυθροδανωμένα (ram skins dyed red) and κατακαλύμματα δέρματα ὑακίνθινα (hyacinth-colored leather coverings) — suggesting the Greek translators understood techash as a blue/violet-colored material rather than a specific animal. The dynamic rendering "fine leather" remains appropriately neutral given the persistent uncertainty, while communicating that this was a high-quality protective outer layer.

### Verses 20–30 — The structural framework
The frames (קְרָשִׁים, qerashim) were the load-bearing skeleton of the tabernacle. Each was ten cubits tall and one and a half cubits wide (approximately 15 feet by 2.25 feet). The word קֶרֶשׁ has been debated — traditional interpretation understood solid wooden planks, but some scholars argue for open frameworks or lattice-like structures (which would be lighter and more portable). Each frame had two יָדוֹת (yadot, literally "hands" — rendered as "tenons"), projections that fitted into the silver אֲדָנִים (adanim, "bases" or "sockets"). The LXX reads στύλους (pillars/posts) for the frames and βάσεις (bases) for the sockets. The forty silver bases on each long side — and the sixteen on the western end — represented an enormous quantity of silver, derived from the half-shekel census contribution (38:25–27). The tabernacle literally stood upon the silver of the entire community.

### Verses 27–29 — The corner frames
The Hebrew description of the corner construction is notoriously difficult. The phrase תֹאֲמִם מִלְּמַטָּה (to'amim millemattah, "twinned from below") suggests the corner frames were paired or doubled from bottom to top, unified at the top into a single ring (טַבַּעַת, tabba'at). Various reconstructions have been proposed, but the basic intent is structural: the corners had to bear stress from two perpendicular walls meeting, requiring reinforced construction. The LXX reads ἐξ ἀμφοτέρων τῶν μερῶν (from both sides), interpreting the pairing as joining two directions. The dynamic rendering "joined together from the bottom all the way to the top, fitted into a single ring" conveys the functional purpose without overcommitting to a specific reconstruction.

### Verse 33 — The middle crossbar
The Hebrew הַבְּרִיחַ הַתִּיכֹן (habberiah hattikon, "the middle bar") ran בְּתוֹךְ הַקְּרָשִׁים (betokh haqqerashim) — either "through the middle of the frames" (i.e., through a channel bored in the wood) or "in the midst of the frames" (i.e., at their midpoint height). The phrase מִן־הַקָּצֶה אֶל־הַקָּצֶה (min-haqqatseh el-haqqatseh, "from end to end") indicates this bar spanned the full length of the wall, providing crucial lateral stability. The other crossbars (five per side) apparently consisted of shorter, overlapping sections. The LXX renders τὸν μοχλὸν τὸν μέσον (the middle bolt/bar). This middle bar, running the entire length, functioned as the structural spine of each wall — holding the whole frame rigid.

### Verses 35–36 — The inner curtain (parokhet)
The פָּרֹכֶת (parokhet) — the inner curtain — separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place. Like the innermost curtains of the tabernacle (v. 8), it featured cherubim worked in by a מַעֲשֵׂה חֹשֵׁב (master designer), and it was made from the same four materials: blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and fine twisted linen. It hung on four gold-overlaid acacia posts set in silver bases. The LXX renders τὸ καταπέτασμα (the veil/curtain). This curtain marked the most sacred boundary in Israel's worship — the threshold beyond which only the high priest could pass, and only once a year on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16). Its later tearing in the New Testament narrative (Matt 27:51) depends on the reader understanding the gravity of what this barrier represented.

### Verses 37–38 — The entrance screen (masakh)
The מָסָךְ (masakh) — the screen at the tent entrance — was made of the same colored yarns and fine linen but was the work of a רֹקֵם (roqem, "embroiderer") rather than a חֹשֵׁב (choshev, "designer"). This represents a lower tier of textile art than the parokhet, reflecting the graded holiness from exterior to interior. Its five posts had gold-overlaid tops and bands but stood in bronze bases — another step down from the silver bases of the inner curtain's posts. The LXX reads ἔργον ποικιλτοῦ (the work of an embroiderer). The entire tabernacle thus embodies a gradient: gold-silver-bronze, designer-embroiderer, Most Holy-Holy-entrance — a spatial theology expressing degrees of nearness to the divine presence.

### Vaticanus Divergences
The LXX in Vaticanus presents perhaps its most significant divergence from the MT in the construction chapters (Exodus 35–40). The Greek tabernacle construction narrative follows a substantially different order from the Hebrew: (1) Vaticanus ch. 36 (in Greek numbering) corresponds not to MT ch. 36 but largely to the tabernacle instructions of MT ch. 26, recounting the curtains, frames, and coverings in a sequence that more closely follows the original instruction order rather than the construction execution order. The MT, by contrast, repeats the construction in an order that closely mirrors the original commands. (2) The surplus passage (MT 36:3–7), which is theologically one of the most striking sections, is present in the LXX tradition but positioned differently. (3) The measurements and structural details are largely consistent between the two traditions, confirming that the divergence is primarily one of arrangement rather than content. (4) Vaticanus uses σκηνή (tent/tabernacle) consistently where the MT alternates between מִשְׁכָּן (mishkan, dwelling) and אֹהֶל (ohel, tent), a distinction the Greek collapses. This broader rearrangement in the LXX construction chapters has been a major topic of text-critical study — some scholars argue the shorter, differently ordered LXX preserves an earlier form of the text, while others maintain the MT's fuller, more repetitive account is original and the LXX reflects deliberate editorial streamlining.
